segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a sweeping aerial view of a vibrant Chinese city at dusk, illuminated by countless lights and crisscrossed by glowing highways, establishing a modern, bustling urban atmosphere, all set to atmospheric, slightly melancholic traditional Chinese-style music featuring string instruments and percussion. A man’s hand gripping a railing suggests movement, transitioning to a futuristic, curved architectural structure and an illuminated modern building, both at night with blurred lights indicating motion. Michael Wood, identified by the BBC logo appearing as he descends an escalator, is first seen walking through a covered walkway adorned with red lanterns. He narrates in a clear British accent, stating that "the past is receding from us at an ever faster rate," especially "here in China." The scene then juxtaposes a large, brightly lit modern river cruise ship with a traditional Chinese-style boat featuring golden dragon embellishments gliding along the river. Michael Wood continues, explaining that "the traveler searching for the meaning of China's ancient culture can still find it in China's present," as "deep currents still shared by all Chinese people" run under the surface. Appearing in a dimly lit setting, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, he emphasizes that "among them is one great stream that has sustained the Chinese across the ages: That's poetry." He asserts that "China has the oldest living tradition of poetry in the world, more than 3,000 years old, older than Homer's Iliad and Odyssey," with on-screen Chinese subtitles reinforcing his narrative as the BBC logo remains visible.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Michael Wood, a middle-aged man with graying hair, wearing a brown jacket over a light blue shirt, speaking expressively with hand gestures in a dimly lit outdoor setting. Identified by on-screen text as the host, he narrates with a clear, articulate voice, stating, "Poets have always been the ones who most truthfully expressed the feelings of the people. And to the Chinese, their greatest poet is Du Fu." Gentle, slightly melancholic background music accompanies his speech as he continues, explaining that Du Fu lived in the 8th century Tang Dynasty, a time of "extraordinary cultural accomplishment that ended in horrendous warfare and the death of millions," and that "out of that suffering, Du Fu created his art." The scene transitions to a panoramic night view of a modern cityscape along a river, featuring illuminated skyscrapers, including a geometrically patterned building with white lighting, and a curved, metallic structure adorned with red lanterns, blending modernity with cultural motifs. A large, multi-decked Modern River Cruise Ship is docked, glowing with blue and white lights and a red neon sign, while a brightly lit, ornate Traditional Chinese-Style Boat with golden dragon head and pagoda-style roofs glides across the water, symbolizing cultural heritage. The music swells slightly before fading as Prof. Stephen Owen, an older man with gray hair wearing a maroon sweater, speaks in a scholarly setting in front of a bookshelf. Identified by on-screen text as a professor from Harvard University, he states with a scholarly tone, "Du Fu belongs to a very special category. We have Dante, Shakespeare, and Du Fu. These poets create the very values by which poetry is judged." The video then transitions to a serene, dimly lit river valley at dusk, with small boats navigating between dark mountains, evoking a sense of historical reflection. Michael Wood is later seen from behind, standing on a rocky outcrop with a Chinese flag beside him, gazing at the mountains, as his narration returns, "In the East, he's one of the immortals. In the West, few people have even heard of him. This is a journey in the footsteps of China's greatest poet." The background music becomes more prominent and cinematic, swelling dramatically with strings and percussion as the final shot shows an ancient-style scroll unfolding over the river, displaying a portrait of Du Fu and calligraphy, symbolizing his enduring legacy.

The scene then shifts to a dark, misty river valley at dusk, where the title 'DU FU — CHINA'S GREATEST POET' appears with Chinese subtitles. The music continues as the view moves to a nighttime Xi'an, showcasing the illuminated Wild Goose Pagoda and the city's name in both English and Chinese. The camera pans across a bustling urban square filled with people dancing and socializing, accompanied by faint crowd chatter under the music. A golden statue of a monk, likely Xuanzang, is then shown in front of the pagoda. A male narrator begins, stating that "Du Fu was born into China's greatest epoch, the Tang Dynasty." As an animated map of the Tang Empire in 712 CE, illustrating the Silk Road and trade routes, appears, the narrator explains that "the Tang had ruled for a century from their capital Chang'an. Today's Xi'an," which was "the greatest city on earth with more than a million people." He continues, describing it as "rich, powerful, and open to the world," a "Golden Age" for the Chinese, concluding that "the catalyst for it all was the Silk Road."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a sepia-toned animated map of the Tang Empire in 712 CE, illustrating vast cultural and trade connections with labeled regions like Persia, India, and the Silk Road. A male narrator, accompanied by an orchestral, slightly dramatic background music, explains how "people, goods, and ideas flowed from India, Iran, and Central Asia into the crowded streets of the capital," establishing the Tang as a "cosmopolitan empire of many cultures and languages." The scene then transitions to a vibrant, bustling modern night market in Xi'an, filled with neon signs advertising Chinese food like '灌汤牛肉面' and '烤面筋'. The lively atmosphere is enhanced by the sounds of distant market chatter, occasional firecrackers, people talking in Chinese, including greetings like "Ni hao," laughter, and a distinct "ding-dong" sound. Crowds navigate narrow alleyways, some with umbrellas, while vendors prepare food and sell colorful trinkets, with a young child sitting among the merchandise. The narrator highlights that in these markets, one could find a a "dazzling mix of foreign fashions and food," including "Persian pistachios and the golden peaches of Samarkand." As the market sounds continue, the narrator introduces the poet Du Fu, whose "extraordinary life story would be entwined forever with the tale of this city and the fate of Tang China itself." The video then shifts to a historical overlay of Emperor Xuanzong (Tang Ming Huang) superimposed over the illuminated Wild Goose Pagoda at dusk, as the narrator reveals that "only a year after Du Fu was born, a new emperor came to the Dragon Throne, Xuanzong, who was nicknamed the Brilliant." The final scene shows a man practicing Chinese calligraphy on a large scroll outdoors, observed by onlookers, with on-screen text describing the Tang Dynasty as a golden age of Chinese culture, emphasizing the enduring legacy of the Tang era in contemporary Xi'an.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a man, a calligraphy artist in a white shirt, deeply focused as he practices Chinese calligraphy, his brush moving deliberately across a large scroll. A male narrator's voice describes this as China's most glorious period of culture and its greatest age of poetry, emphasizing that poetry gives the truest history of the human heart, all set to background music. The scene transitions to a wider shot of a calligraphy artist at work with inkstone and brush, writing bold, flowing characters, then to a young boy with glasses meticulously copying calligraphy from a book, and finally to an artist in a traditional Chinese studio adorned with artworks. The setting then shifts outdoors to a serene, green environment where Prof. Zeng Xiangbo from Renmin University, a bald man with glasses, speaks in Chinese. He explains that the Chinese nation pays attention to historical records, and Du Fu's poetry is the best work combining history with personal and spiritual journeys. As the video transitions to aerial views of Du Fu’s birthplace in Gongyi, showcasing traditional Chinese architecture with tiled roofs and courtyards, the distinct sound of flowing water becomes prominent. A man walks through the complex, and the camera pans over the historic site, including a stone gate with golden characters and a large inscribed boulder. The English narrator returns, explaining that the Du family were an old upper-middle-class clan of officials and poets, and in Imperial China, these roles were intertwined, requiring proven literary skills, while the flowing water sound and background music continue. On-screen text in both English and Chinese provides further context about Du Fu’s life and the importance of historical recording.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Michael Wood's feet walking on stone steps beside a weathered brick wall, accompanied by the sound of his footsteps. He narrates in a clear, British accent that the family house is long gone and this is all that's left. He then enters a dimly lit, arched doorway, stepping into a shadowy interior. As he approaches a white statue of a robed figure, likely Du Fu, placed on a pedestal with red lotus flowers and lit candles, he states that tradition says Du Fu was born "here in this room," with the distinct sound of a door creaking and a distant gong. The scene shifts to Michael Wood, now outside a traditional brick building with a dark wooden door, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. He recounts a mysterious story: when Du Fu was a baby, the region was struck by plague, causing widespread deaths. One day, a sorceress or shamaness visited the house and looked in the bedroom, where two babies were asleep—his aunt's child and little Du Fu. The sorceress prophesied that only one of these children would survive, specifically the one sleeping in the southeast corner of the room. The aunt then took her own child out of that corner and placed Du Fu there, leading to his survival. The natural, overcast lighting emphasizes the somber tone of his narrative, which is accompanied by on-screen Chinese subtitles.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Michael Wood, wearing a brown jacket, speaking expressively in front of a weathered brick wall and dark wooden door, narrating in a clear voice, "And for that reason, says Du Fu, my aunt's son died, and I lived." Subtle, melancholic background music accompanies his words. The scene transitions to a dimly lit historical reenactment, showing a young boy in traditional Chinese attire smiling and gesturing upward, as Michael Wood continues, "So maybe, even as a child, he felt as if he had the mark of destiny on him." This is followed by a close-up of a young girl in traditional clothing, focused on writing or reading at a wooden desk, partially obscured by bamboo bars, while Michael Wood explains, "More than 1400 of his poems survive, a uniquely intimate autobiography." The music swells slightly. The video then cuts to a black-and-white ink drawing of an elderly man, likely Du Fu, with Chinese calligraphy, before shifting to Prof. Stephen Owen, an older man with gray hair and a teal scarf, speaking solemnly against a dark background. On-screen text identifies 'My Brave Adventures' as Michael Wood narrates, "Years later, as an old man, he wrote the story of his childhood and youth in a poem spoken by Ian McKellen." A distinct, deep, and resonant voice (Ian McKellen) then recites Du Fu's poetry: "When I was still only in my seventh year, my mind was already full of heroic deeds. My first poem was about the Phoenix... a new age of wisdom. When I was in my ninth year, I'd already written enough poems to fill a satchel." During this recitation, the final shot shows Michael Wood walking down a tree-lined path in a park, looking upward, with the camera tracking him from a low angle, as Chinese subtitles display the poetic lines, and the background music becomes more prominent and cinematic.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a dynamic bronze sculpture depicting two figures on galloping horses, set in a lush green park with manicured hedges and trees. As subtle traditional Chinese instrumental music plays, Prof. Stephen Owen's solemn, measured voice begins reciting lines from Du Fu's poem 'My Brave Adventures', stating, "At 14, I first began to read my poems in public. Literary masters compared me to the great writers of the past." The camera then zooms in on the sculpture, emphasizing its movement and energy. The scene transitions to Prof. Stephen Owen, an older man with gray hair, wearing a black shirt and teal scarf, speaking solemnly in front of a dark, textured background. He continues his recitation, with Chinese subtitles appearing, saying, "I was temperamental and I was already over fond of wine. I needed it to soften an uncompromising hatred of wickedness and hypocrisy." An aerial view of a traditional Chinese architectural complex with gray-tiled roofs, red gates, and symmetrical courtyards, surrounded by greenery, then appears, showing a lone figure walking along a central path, while Prof. Owen's voice continues, "I associated only with wise old gray heads. Exhilarated by wine, we cast our glances over the entire universe. And all vulgar worldliness dwindled into oblivion." The camera returns to Prof. Stephen Owen before shifting to Michael Wood, a middle-aged man with graying hair, wearing a brown jacket over a light blue shirt. He walks through a traditional courtyard with red walls and green foliage, then stands in front of a small pavilion, speaking expressively with hand gestures and a reflective tone. He comments, "I find that portrait of the artist as a young man, written by the older Du Fu, really authentic. He obviously felt even by the age of 14 that he was different from his schoolmates and his contemporaries. Um, maybe also..." The serene, historically evocative setting with natural light and traditional architecture enhances the contemplative mood.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a traditional Chinese stone archway, inscribed with red characters, marking the entrance to Qufu, Confucius’ hometown. A man, identified as Michael Wood, walks toward the archway, dressed in a dark jacket over a light blue shirt, carrying a black bag, as a traditional, almost liturgical chanting sound is heard in the background. Michael Wood's articulate voice then begins to narrate, introducing Qufu as the birthplace of the philosopher Confucius, who shaped the core values of Chinese civilization over a thousand years before. The scene transitions to a low-angle view of towering trees and a traditional Chinese temple with red walls and ornate tiled roofs, emphasizing the historical and spiritual significance of the location. A close-up reveals a bronze statue of Confucius with a long beard, hands clasped in a gesture of wisdom, set against a blue background with golden Chinese characters, as Wood's narration continues, explaining Confucius's belief that the main goal of life is social harmony and stability under a virtuous ruler, with each person playing a role in achieving the common good. Michael Wood is then seen walking along a red-painted wall with decorative panels, stopping to touch a stone tablet inscribed with classical Chinese calligraphy, accompanied by a distinct sound of wood or stone. The camera zooms in on the tablet, highlighting the intricate carvings and text, which embody Confucian values like benevolence, righteousness, and loyalty. Finally, Michael Wood stands before a traditional wooden lattice door, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, reflecting on how Confucius's teachings—virtue, benevolence, and service to the state—were guiding principles and how his thought continues to influence Chinese political tradition even today. The lighting is natural and bright throughout, conveying an atmosphere of reverence and scholarly reflection.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an aerial view of a lush green landscape, overlaid with an animated map tracing Du Fu's journey from Luoyang to Xi'an in 735 CE, marked by a red arrow along the Yellow River. This transitions to a modern-day aerial shot of Xi'an's bustling cityscape, centered on the iconic Bell Tower, with traffic flowing along a wide avenue lined with trees and red banners. The camera then moves to a high-angle view from the ancient city wall, showing a traditional pavilion and modern buildings in the hazy background, as Michael Wood, a middle-aged man in a light blue shirt, walks along the stone-paved wall. Speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, his clear, informative voice narrates Du Fu's utopian aspirations: "So he went to the capital, Changan, today's Xi'an, expecting to become a government official. It was his chance to serve the brilliant emperor and help continue the new age of wisdom." As he speaks, the sounds of bustling city traffic, including car horns, become more prominent, underscoring the vibrant modern environment. Michael Wood continues, his voice becoming more emphatic, describing Changan as "the city of peace... a world city in the 8th century, the symbol of all that human civilization had to offer." He emphasizes the city's "powerful presence in Du Fu's imagination all his life," representing "the dream of success and power and fame." The camera pans to reveal the multi-tiered, beige Wild Goose Pagoda rising above traditional rooftops and greenery, with modern high-rises visible in the distance, reinforcing Xi'an’s blend of ancient heritage and contemporary urban development. Michael Wood concludes, "And in his early 20s, he came here to sit the imperial examinations, confident that a glittering career lay ahead of him." The segment ends with a wide shot of the pagoda and its surrounding courtyard, symbolizing its deep imprint in Du Fu’s imagination.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a sweeping low-angle shot of a grand, ornate traditional Chinese-style archway labeled 'LUOYANG,' its multi-tiered roof and stone structure prominent under a bright sky, as melancholic traditional Chinese string music begins to play. The camera then moves through the arch into a bustling ancient street, vibrant with shops, colorful banners, and people walking, riding scooters, and bicycles. A male narrator's voice explains that an exam failure "cut him loose from the career ladder," causing him to wander and "hate himself for living on the coattails of the rich." Amidst the lively street, a man on a scooter approaches the camera, and a calligraphy artist practices on a large scroll outdoors, observed by onlookers. The scene shifts to Prof. Stephen Owen, an older man with gray hair, wearing a black shirt and teal scarf, speaking solemnly in front of a dark background. His deeper, resonant voice recites lines from Du Fu's poetry, describing how "The next few years, I played and roamed," singing in spring, hunting in summer, and whistling to falcons in winter, chasing wild beasts. Chinese subtitles accompany his recitation. The video cuts back to the street, showing a vendor cooking steaming food at a stall, followed by a wide, elevated shot of the crowded street, emphasizing its dynamic energy. Prof. Owen's voice continues, recounting how he "trailed my hems wherever sweet ale was served, and drank myself sick," as the background music builds in intensity. Finally, an animated portrait of a Tang Dynasty man, identified as Du Fu, appears with Chinese text. The original narrator's voice returns, explaining that when Du Fu was about 30, "came the meeting that changed his life with the poet Li Bai," as the music fades, and a close-up of the animated portrait confirms Li Bai as the greatest poet of his time.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an animated portrait of a Tang Dynasty man, identified as Li Bai, depicted with a black cap, long beard, and white robe against a solid red background. Chinese subtitles describe him as 'the Bright Star' and highlight his charismatic, drunken, and rebellious nature, as Michael Wood's clear, narrative English voice introduces him as "a great poet of the time," and quotes Du Fu calling him "the star of the Golden Gate." Wood continues, describing Li Bai as "charismatic, drunkard, brawler," a "frequenter of the blue houses" who "killed men in fights," but also "a brilliant, innovative, scintillating poet." The scene transitions to Michael Wood, seated at a wooden table in a bustling outdoor market, wearing a light blue shirt and gesturing expressively as he speaks, with the lively sounds of the market, including pedestrians and vendors, audible in the background. He narrates how Du Fu "fell under his spell," quoting Du Fu's admiration: "What wonderful verses he writes," and "I love him like a brother." Wood continues, quoting Du Fu's recollection of their travels: "Those nights we traveled, we slept under the same quilt, and every day we walked together hand-in-hand," adding that they "didn't think about jobs in the government," but "only thought of other-worldly things." The camera then cuts to a small bookstore or stationery shop, where a man in a plaid shirt is writing on paper while another man browses books, surrounded by shelves stacked with books, calligraphy supplies, and traditional items, including a sign reading '唐诗一万首' (Ten Thousand Tang Poems). A close-up shows a hand writing Chinese calligraphy with a brush, emphasizing the artistic and literary theme. The scene returns briefly to Michael Wood, continuing his narration, with Chinese subtitles displaying lines like '李侯有佳句，往往似阴铿' and '醉眠秋共被，携手日同行'. The segment ends with a blurred, misty shot of mountains, accompanied by a shift from market sounds to a melancholic, traditional Chinese-sounding instrumental music. This is followed by a final close-up of a brush writing calligraphy, symbolizing the enduring legacy of Tang poetry and the emotional depth of the poets’ friendship, as Michael Wood's voice briefly returns to say, "Li Bai was Du Fu's..." before the segment concludes.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a serene, misty river scene at dawn or dusk, showcasing traditional Chinese architecture with tiered roofs and ornate details against hazy mountains, all set to a melancholic traditional Chinese instrumental score. A close-up then shows a calligraphy brush moving swiftly across paper, creating bold, flowing Chinese characters. The scene transitions to an outdoor interview where Prof. Zeng Xiangbo, a bald man in a light blue shirt and glasses, speaks animatedly in Chinese, explaining how Li Bai inspired Du Fu in poetry and storytelling, particularly in "narrative art and detail portrayal." He is conversing with a Western man in a black suit, identified as Michael Wood, who earlier introduced their bond as "the most important creative relationship" and later reflects in English that "for a time, the friendship was close... but then they split up, and he never saw Li Bai again, although he still dreamed of him." The video returns to the misty river landscape, reinforcing the emotional weight of their separation. The final segment features Prof. Stephen Owen, an older man with gray hair wearing a black shirt and teal scarf, standing solemnly against a dark, textured background as he recites poignant lines from Du Fu’s poem 'Dreaming of Li Bai' in English, stating, "Separation in life is a continuing grief. No word from your old friend. But you've been in my dreams, as if you know how much I miss you," with Chinese subtitles appearing on screen, maintaining the reflective and melancholic mood.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a traditional Chinese ink brush dipping into ink and elegantly forming calligraphy on paper, displaying Chinese subtitles '水深波浪阔 无使蛟龙得' (Deep waters, wide waves, do not let the dragon get away). A male narrator's voice translates these lines, stating, "The waters are deep, the waves are wide. Don't let the river gods take you," accompanied by atmospheric, traditional music. The scene transitions to a serene, mist-covered river at dawn, reflecting traditional Chinese architecture and distant hills, as the calligraphy is completed with a red seal. The narrator continues, "It was an eerie premonition. For Li Bai would drown on a drunken boating trip, but he showed Du Fu what poetry could be." The setting then shifts indoors to a room with teal walls and large windows, where Michael Wood, a middle-aged man with graying hair in a dark suit, sits opposite Dr. Tao Tao Liu, an older woman with short gray hair in a black cardigan. Michael Wood, speaking with a clear British accent, initiates a scholarly discussion, asking, "It's amazing, isn't it? China's two greatest poets lived at the same time and knew each other. What effect did that relationship have on Du Fu, do you think?" Dr. Liu, gesturing with her hands and speaking with a calm, scholarly accent, explains, "Li Bo or Li Bai, Li Bo was the old pronunciation. Had a character that was very charismatic. And a lot of people were attracted to him. And and and Du Fu worshipped him. But his poetry is not at all like that of Li Bai's," with Michael Wood briefly interjecting, "That's almost..." The soft, natural lighting enhances the intellectual ambiance as Michael Wood listens intently, nodding.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Michael Wood, seated at a wooden table in a teal-walled room, wearing a dark suit, asking Dr. Tao Tao Liu, "Tell us, tell us about the difference." Dr. Tao Tao Liu, an older woman with short gray hair in a black knitted cardigan, responds articulately, using hand gestures to explain that while Li Bai focused on being at one with nature, Du Fu was more concerned with people and the importance of living according to Confucianism, specifically emphasizing the need to be "loyal to your emperor" or "the leader you chose," as "the leadership was very important to Confucianism." The scene then transitions to a grand 3D animated reconstruction of the Daming Palace, featuring beige walls and imperial flags. Michael Wood, now in a brown jacket, walks through the animated courtyard, narrating in a clear, engaging voice that it was "very important to Du Fu" to serve the empire. He describes Du Fu settling in the capital, failing exams, but "kept writing," eventually sending his work "through a friend... directly to the emperor" at the Daming Palace. Michael continues, explaining that "to his astonishment, the emperor read them, and summoned him," quoting Du Fu's words, "The Son of Heaven summoned me to his presence," as officials are depicted arriving.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Michael Wood, wearing a brown jacket over a light blue shirt, standing before an animated reconstruction of the Daming Palace. He gestures as he narrates Du Fu’s words in a clear, authoritative voice, describing his amazement at his sudden brilliant reputation after entering court. The camera then cuts to a wide shot of the Daming Palace’s grand facade, emphasizing its imposing architecture with multiple arched doors and a yellow banner. Michael Wood's narration continues, explaining that Du Fu finally had a job at court, but despite enabling him to marry, it was a dead end, leading to his disillusionment. A dramatic reenactment unfolds inside a dimly lit imperial hall, where a figure in ornate robes walks down a patterned carpet, flanked by bowing court officials, toward the emperor seated on a throne, underscoring the court's hierarchical structure. Subtitles appear, quoting Du Fu's feeling of having 'no role at all in court discussions,' merely one of thousands serving the emperor's every whim. The scene shifts to a modern interview setting with teal walls and large windows, where Dr. Tao Tao Liu, in a black knitted cardigan over a beige collared shirt, speaks thoughtfully and with a distinct accent about Du Fu's inability to fit into civil service. Michael Wood, now in a dark suit over a light blue shirt, listens intently, nodding and smiling slightly. Dr. Liu explains that Du Fu was 'too honest a character' to bow to court demands, emphasizing his desire to be a morally influential figure rather than a mere servant. The segment concludes with Dr. Tao Tao Liu stressing the importance of Confucian loyalty to a leader, reinforcing the cultural and ethical context of Du Fu’s life and poetry.

A regal emperor with a mustache and elaborate golden crown sits on a throne, flanked by a beaded curtain and a court lady in ornate attire. The emperor is shown receiving a scroll, symbolizing governance and power, as the narrator explains that for Du Fu, "the closer he got to the throne, the clearer he saw the reality of power." The narrative then shifts to Prof. Stephen Owen, an older man with gray hair, wearing a black shirt and teal scarf, standing solemnly in front of a dark, textured background, reciting lines from Du Fu’s poem 'Zhu You' with Chinese subtitles appearing on screen. He speaks of a time when "the powerful indulged in murder and plunder," and how "many warnings could have been obtained from history about why a dynasty falls." The segment concludes with a sweeping upward shot of traditional Chinese architecture, revealing the Wild Goose Pagoda rising above tiled rooftops under a clear sky, symbolizing the enduring legacy of the Tang Dynasty and the historical weight of its decline, while the narrator reflects that "through all of Chinese history, the problem has been the same," due to the country's vast size.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a low-angle shot of the multi-tiered, beige Wild Goose Pagoda in Xi'an, framed by dark traditional eaves against a pale sky, as Michael Wood, in a dark suit, walks up stone steps towards it, his back to the camera. His somber voice narrates, "lose their grip, things fall apart," followed by a deep gong sound. As he ascends, the camera pans to reveal the pagoda's full height, partially obscured by lush green trees. Michael Wood then stands before the pagoda, speaking directly to the camera, stating, "And it was at this point that Du Fu had a disturbing experience. What almost sounds like a sudden seizure," punctuated by another gong. He continues, "Du Fu came with three friends here to the Wild Goose Pagoda in Xi'an. They went inside and climbed up to the top to take the view at sunset." Chinese subtitles quoting Du Fu’s poem 'Tong Zhe Gong Deng Cizin Si Ta' appear. The scene transitions to a sepia-toned, animated aerial view of an ancient imperial complex, likely the Daming Palace, with symmetrical courtyards and traditional buildings stretching into the distance. Michael Wood's voice describes Du Fu's experience: "But when he looked out, Du Fu was seized by a kind of existential panic. The mountains of the north began to dissolve. The Way River disappeared." An eerie wind sound accompanies the visual as he adds, "And then the imperial city itself vanished in a kind of haze." The camera slowly zooms out, revealing the vast scale of the palace under a hazy sky, while Chinese subtitles continue to display lines from Du Fu’s poem, reflecting themes of fragmentation and existential dread. Michael Wood concludes, "And the wild geese flew away," as distinct sounds of geese honking and flapping wings, combined with wind and a mournful drone, fill the audio. The segment ends with a close-up of a traditional tiled roof, then a shot of a red wooden door with intricate carvings, as the camera moves through the entrance, suggesting a transition into the historical narrative of the empire’s decline, with Michael Wood's voice cut off mid-sentence: "The empire now went into..." The atmosphere is somber and reflective, blending real-world visuals with animated reconstructions to convey the emotional and political turmoil Du Fu experienced.

The camera then focuses on a large, ornate pavilion by the water, framed by mountains and a stone bridge, as Prof. Owen continues, "Here at the hot springs, the emperor entertains his court, and music echoes around the hills." The scene cuts to Prof. Stephen Owen, an older man with gray hair, wearing a black shirt and teal scarf, speaking solemnly in front of a dark, textured background, reciting lines from Du Fu’s poem with Chinese subtitles appearing on screen: "Only the rich and powerful may bathe here, but the silk they wear was woven by poor people, women whose husbands are beaten for their taxes." The narrative shifts to an animated sequence showing two figures in Tang Dynasty attire—a man in a golden robe and a woman in red—walking between red pillars, gazing out over a cityscape, suggesting imperial life and courtly elegance. The camera captures their profiles as they stand together, then turns to show their faces, emphasizing their regal presence, while Prof. Owen describes, "The halls are full of ladies as fair as goddesses. Scent of perfume moves with each captivating figure, clothed in the warm furs of sable." The scene returns to Prof. Stephen Owen, continuing his recitation, "Entertained with the finest music, fed with camel hump soup and oranges ripened in the frost." The segment concludes with a dark, stormy view of a traditional Chinese gate flanked by stone lions, with the sign '紫禁城' (Forbidden City) visible, symbolizing imperial power and the stark contrast between opulence and suffering, as highlighted by the subtitle '朱门酒肉臭，路有冻死骨' (At noble gates, wine and meat stink; on the road, frozen bones lie), while Prof. Owen recites, "Behind the red lacquered gates, wine is left to sour, meat to rot. The overall tone is somber and reflective, juxtaposing the beauty of Tang architecture and court life with the harsh realities of social inequality, as conveyed through the poetry and visual storytelling, all accompanied by the melancholic music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic, desaturated shot of a traditional Chinese architectural complex under a stormy sky, featuring stone lion statues and snow-covered rooftops, evoking a somber historical atmosphere, accompanied by melancholic music. Prof. Stephen Owen, an older man with gray hair, wearing a black shirt and teal scarf, appears solemnly in front of a dark, textured backdrop, reciting poignant lines from Du Fu’s poem about the “bones of the frozen and the starved,” with Chinese subtitles. The scene transitions to misty, snow-laden mountain peaks, reinforcing a sense of isolation and hardship, as a narrator explains that “when Du Fu got to the village, his worst fears had come true.” Prof. Owen continues his recitation, describing the wailing in the house and the death of an infant child from hunger, expressing the poet's guilt: “I am ashamed of being a father, so poor that I caused my son to die for lack of food.” He then questions the suffering of the common people if even his privileged life is so bitter. The visuals shift to a bleak, snow-covered village with crumbling brick walls and icy doorways, visually representing the poem’s themes of suffering. The scene then cuts to a modern-day view of the Wild Goose Pagoda at dusk, juxtaposing ancient heritage with contemporary urban life. A sudden dramatic sound effect and a shift to a chaotic, animated battle sequence, filled with the sounds of horse hooves and clashing, show mounted warriors charging through a courtyard, signaling the outbreak of rebellion. The narrator declares, “Worse was to come. In mid-December 755, a huge rebellion broke out, led by the renegade general...” The segment concludes with a close-up of a traditional painted portrait of a bearded general in armor, identified by the narrator as An Lushan, the rebel leader, with Chinese subtitles explaining his military prowess and the timing of the revolt in December 755 CE. The overall narrative moves from poetic lament to historical upheaval, using visual contrasts and authoritative narration to convey the collapse of peace and the onset of war.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic animated battle scene, accompanied by intense battle sound effects, depicting mounted warriors charging through a dusty, war-torn landscape with fallen soldiers and debris, symbolizing the chaos of the 755 CE rebellion led by An Lushan, whose army of a quarter of a million men marched on the capital, as narrated by Michael Wood. The animation transitions to a real-world interview setting where Dr. Tao Tao Liu, an older woman with short gray hair wearing a black knitted cardigan, speaks thoughtfully, gesturing as she emphasizes that “755 is the date I always tell everybody, when the whole of China collapsed.” She continues, explaining how the rebellion “floored the empire completely,” leading to the emperor's resignation, his son taking over, and “a lot of infighting.” Michael Wood, a middle-aged man in a dark suit, listens intently. Dr. Liu further details how the country was “completely let go of” and there was “a huge drive for recruitment of soldiers on either side,” concluding that it was “very hard for ordinary people to live and to survive.” The video then shifts to a panoramic view of the Ancient City Wall of Xi'an, where modern buildings rise behind the historic stone wall and people walk and cycle along the top. Overlaid animated sequences of soldiers and horses charging across the wall, accompanied by dramatic music and battle sounds, visually represent the destruction and displacement. Michael Wood's narration returns, explaining that “The crisis lasted eight years. The destruction and loss of life was huge.” Chinese subtitles appear, reinforcing his statement that Tang government censuses suggest “as many as 30 million people were displaced, killed in war, or died of famine,” blending past trauma with present-day continuity.

The animation transitions to a traditional Chinese-style courtyard where figures in Tang Dynasty attire flee in panic, as the narrator continues, "As the rebels closed in on the capital, Du Fu and his family took to the road, heading north to escape the fighting." Brief sounds of a horse neighing and distant battle clashing underscore the turmoil. The scene shifts to a modern-day setting, where Michael Wood walks along a rural path, his footsteps clearly audible, evoking the arduous journey described in Du Fu’s poetry. Chinese subtitles quote lines from 'Ballad of Pengya Road' as he walks through fields and past a dilapidated brick wall. Prof. Stephen Owen then appears, standing solemnly against a dark, textured background, his deep, resonant voice reciting Du Fu’s verses: "I remember when we first fled the rebels, hurrying north over dangerous trails... We walked for ten days, holding hands, half in rain and thunder, through the mud we pulled each other on. Searching the horizon for a wisp of smoke that might lead us to a safe shelter." Chinese subtitles accompany his recitation. The segment concludes with a shot of a crumbling mud-brick house, its cracked walls symbolizing the decay of society and the personal suffering endured during the war, all set to a somber, reflective musical score.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a Crumbled Mud-Brick House, its walls cracked and weathered, symbolizing decay and hardship, with green foliage encroaching around it, all set to a gentle, melancholic background music. Michael Wood's calm, narrative voice begins, explaining that "The war was the turning point in Du Fu's life, and the great divide in his poetry. Now he knew what it meant to be, as Shakespeare would put it, a poor, unaccommodated man." The scene transitions to a rural path where Michael Wood walks, his footsteps shown in close-up, evoking the arduous journey described in Du Fu’s poetry. He moves through a village with traditional mud-brick homes and a dirt path lined with banana trees. A Motorbike with a Red Cover speeds past, kicking up dust, its distinct engine sound heard. The narrative shifts to Prof. Yang Yu from Central South University, standing in a dry, grassy field, speaking with expressive hand gestures in Chinese. Her voice, accompanied by the continuing melancholic music, elaborates on Du Fu’s transformation from a privileged poet to a man struggling with poverty and displacement, and how his poetry became "heavier and more realistic." As she finishes, a dramatic, tense music swells with a metallic clang, and Michael Wood's voice returns, narrating, "In spring 756, the capital fell to An Lushan's army." The scene then cuts to a modern urban setting with a large Bronze Sculpture of Riders on Horses in front of the Wild Goose Pagoda, symbolizing historical conflict. The camera pans across the pagoda and a nearby moat, with a stone tower on a fortified wall, as Chinese subtitles describe the fall of the capital in 756 CE to An Lushan’s forces and Du Fu’s capture en route to join the fleeing court. Michael Wood continues, "The new emperor and his court had fled south, but when Du Fu tried to join them, he was captured on the road by the rebels," with the dramatic music intensifying before cutting off, emphasizing the weight of Du Fu's life-changing experiences.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of the Ancient City Wall of Xi'an, a massive stone structure with a traditional multi-tiered pavilion, flanked by green trees and red flags under an overcast sky. A male narrator explains that Du Fu was 'taken as a captive to the capital' and 'once more separated from his family,' accompanied by somber traditional Chinese music, while Chinese subtitles reference Du Fu’s poetry and his family separation. The scene transitions to dark, moody willow branches silhouetted against a dim sky, displaying the text 'MOON NIGHT' and a poetic line from Du Fu’s 'Moon Night', as the melancholic music swells. A narrow, dimly lit alley at night, illuminated by a single warm window light, evokes solitude before an animated, sepia-toned scroll unfolds, showing a stylized portrait of a Tang Dynasty man, likely Du Fu, with Chinese calligraphy and poetic lines. During these scenes, Prof. Stephen Owen, an older man with gray hair, wearing a black shirt and teal scarf, is shown speaking solemnly, reciting lines from Du Fu’s 'Moon Night' in a clear, deliberate voice, including 'The moon shines in Fuzhou tonight,' 'In her chamber she watches alone,' 'Her cloud-like hair sweet with mist, her jade arms cold in the clear moonlight,' and 'When shall we lean in the empty window together in brightness, our tears dried up?' His recitation is underscored by the soft, melancholic music, with Chinese subtitles appearing on screen. The video then transitions to a bustling modern street in China, with shops, Chinese signage, and pedestrians, as the traditional music fades and is replaced by the sounds of urban life, including traffic, chatter, and a distinct vehicle horn. A man is seen loading brooms onto a cart, and a bald man in a blue shirt handles the brooms, symbolizing manual labor. The narrator explains that Du Fu 'spent that winter and the following spring doing forced labor as a porter under rebel rule in the ruined capital,' but 'he was still writing poetry.' The final shot focuses on the man’s hands arranging the brooms, emphasizing his toil, with Chinese subtitles describing Du Fu’s post-rebellion life as a laborer.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Dr. Tao Tao Liu, an older woman with short gray hair in a black knitted cardigan, seated at a wooden table with teal paneled walls, speaking thoughtfully. She explains in a clear voice how one identifies with Du Fu as a "helpless pawn" amidst great events, emphasizing that he "couldn't help writing" and that it was at this time his "art really grows" beyond an ordinary person's. Michael Wood, a middle-aged man with graying hair, listens intently and responds with a warm voice, reinforcing that Du Fu "becomes a great poet through the experience of war and suffering." Dr. Liu agrees, stating emphatically that "without the An Lushan, we wouldn't have Du Fu as we know him now." A solemn, melancholic background music begins as the scene transitions to an exterior shot of the multi-tiered Wild Goose Pagoda, illuminated at dusk amidst modern city lights, followed by a close-up of a traditional Chinese temple roof with a golden '大雄宝殿' plaque. A man in a black shirt is then seen bowing reverently before a lit candle. A male narrator's voice, distinct from the previous speakers, explains that "It was at this time that Du Fu wrote one of his most celebrated poems." Finally, Prof. Stephen Owen, an older man with gray hair and a teal scarf, solemnly recites lines from Du Fu’s 'Spring Scene' in English, with Chinese subtitles appearing: "The state is destroyed, but the country remains. In the city in spring, grass and weeds grow everywhere." The scene concludes with a close-up of red candles melting, their dripping wax underscoring the poem's emotional weight, as the solemn music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up through a dark metal lattice, revealing a person in a light gray robe tending to greenery behind red lanterns, evoking a serene, traditional setting. This transitions to a close-up of burning incense sticks with golden and black paper wrappings, some charred at the tips, symbolizing prayer or remembrance. A solemn male voice, belonging to Michael Wood, recites lines from Du Fu's poetry: "Grieving for the times, even the blossom sheds tears. Beacon fires have been burning for three months now. A letter from home would be worth 10,000 in gold." The scene then shifts to a moving perspective from a train window, showing blurred green landscapes as Michael Wood, a man with graying hair, looks out thoughtfully. The rhythmic sounds of the train become audible as Michael Wood's voice transitions to narration, explaining Du Fu's escape through rebel lines and his family's journey westward in winter 759 CE, driven by starvation. An animated map of the Tang Empire overlays the train window, tracing the 'Family Escape by Road' from Xi’an to Tianshui and then south to Chengdu, illustrating the perilous mountainous route. Michael Wood continues to narrate the "terrible journey over the forbidding mountainous divide of Central China" down to Sichuan and Chengdu. The scene cuts to Michael Wood walking through a traditional Chinese archway labeled '锦里' (Jinli), entering a bustling, crowded street market filled with people, vendors, and greenery. The train sounds are replaced by the lively ambiance of the market, with chatter and distant calls. Michael Wood concludes, expressing, "You can feel his relief to be down south. Suddenly, I'm under a new corner..." The final shot shows a man in a black jacket and sunglasses near a wooden-framed stall, adding to the vibrant market atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a person raking grains on sunlit, dusty ground, their manual labor emphasized by the distinct, rhythmic sound of raking. A male voice, identified through Chinese subtitles as commentary on Du Fu, begins speaking, noting that Du Fu has often been portrayed as a Confucian hero, yet many of his poems focus on ordinary people and the simple joys of life. The scene transitions to a man and a woman working together outdoors, using a long-handled rake and a wooden winnowing tool to separate grain from chaff, against a backdrop of greenery and a rural path, with the continuous sound of raking and grain processing. The male voice continues, referencing Chinese critics who believed Du Fu never forgot the empire or suffering people, but then challenges this, suggesting Du Fu would forget such concerns when enjoying a good meal, concentrating entirely on it. Visually, the woman pours grain into a wooden thresher while the man operates it, sending a golden stream of grains cascading out. A baby crawls on the ground, reaching for the scattered grains, symbolizing innocence. The male voice highlights Du Fu's unique focus on food in his poetry, describing a sensuality missing in traditional Confucian hero narratives. The scene shifts to a woman carrying a large bundle of straw on her back, walking along a rural path, reinforcing the theme of rural life. The male voice then explains that in Chengdu, Du Fu transformed into a 'poet farmer,' finding beauty in nature and the intimacy of everyday life. The camera pans to a serene river with bare branches, accompanied by the distinct sound of flowing water. A man is seen writing in a notebook by the riverbank, suggesting poetic reflection. The final shot shows a group of four women sitting on the rocky riverside, facing the water, engaged in quiet conversation or contemplation with lush green hills in the background, as the sound of flowing water persists. The overall lighting is warm and natural, evoking a peaceful, pastoral atmosphere, while the Chinese subtitles consistently reinforce the themes of Du Fu's connection to nature, daily life, and sensory pleasures.

The scene transitions to a woman in a beige jacket and dark pants walking briskly along a dirt path beside a stone wall, carrying a bundle on her shoulder, suggesting rural labor, while Wood continues to explain that grieving can be for an ideal or civilization, drawing an analogy to Sigmund Freud. Michael Wood then appears in a verdant garden with tall bamboo stalks behind him, wearing a light brown jacket over a blue shirt, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, connecting these ideas to Du Fu's 'cultural sorrow.' A dramatic musical sting accompanies a shift to a low-angle shot looking up through dense bamboo, followed by a sweeping view of misty, cloud-covered mountains, over which Wood narrates the collapse of 'all the certainties of the old world' in the face of total war, and the death of the new emperor in spring 762. The visuals culminate in a dynamic, animated battle scene with dark silhouettes of cavalry charging across a battlefield, symbolizing the Tibetan invasion into Western China, underscored by intense battle sounds of horse whinnies, clashing, and shouts, as Wood describes the Tibetans seizing their moment to launch a massive raid.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic orchestral score featuring traditional Chinese elements and a gong sound. Michael Wood, a middle-aged man with graying hair, is seen walking along a covered walkway at night on a modern river cruise ship, carrying a black backpack with yellow zippers. He moves past illuminated railings and red lanterns, transitioning from the ship’s exterior to its interior corridor, where he walks past a blue netted barrier and a fire alarm. He then steps onto the ship’s deck, gazing out at a river reflecting golden lights from a distant, illuminated traditional Chinese-style bridge and pagoda. Michael Wood narrates in a clear, authoritative British accent, explaining that "As their armies advanced in the west, and the rebels ravaged the north, Du Fu took to the road again, now in increasingly bad health," adding that "The life of a poor refugee had taken its toll." Subtitles on screen reference Du Fu’s poetic lament about the uncertain future and his inability to return to Chang’an, which Michael Wood quotes: "The road ahead is blurred and lost," and "Where is the capital now?" He concludes, "He would never see it again." The scene shifts to daytime, with the distinct hum of a ship's engine/propeller heard as the vessel moves through a narrow, green river valley flanked by steep, rocky cliffs. Michael Wood is seen from behind, seated in the ship’s cabin, observing the river through the window while holding a microphone. He continues his narration, stating, "So now we follow his track onwards, on what the Chinese call the Great River," and, "Leaving the heartland of China behind, he heads east on the Yangtze, still a great artery..." A brief, muffled sound of distant chatter is heard before fading. The camera pans across the lush, rugged landscape, highlighting the enduring natural beauty and historical significance of the waterway, which Michael Wood emphasizes as a vital artery for commerce and communication, mirroring Du Fu’s historical journey down the Yangtze.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with sweeping aerial shots of the Yangtze River winding through towering, verdant mountains, emphasizing its historical role as a vital artery for "communication and commerce, as it was in his time." Michael Wood, a middle-aged man with graying hair, wearing a light brown jacket over a blue shirt and carrying a black backpack with yellow zippers, stands on the deck of a modern river cruise ship, narrating with a clear, engaging voice. He describes the river as offering "a little glimpse of the old-fashioned travel on the river Yangtze," explaining that "in the Tang Dynasty, they say thousands of ships were going up and down every day. And the nation would come to a halt if the ships stopped." The scene transitions to wide river views, showing the ship moving through narrow gorges, its engine audibly humming. A narrator's voice explains that "over the next few months, he sailed 400 miles downriver to Kuizhou, today's Baidicheng." A graphic overlay appears, displaying an animated map titled 'ESCAPE TO BAIDICHENG 762–766 CE,' tracing Du Fu’s route from Chengdu to Luzhou and then to Baidicheng, with key cities like Xi’an and Tianshui labeled. The narrator continues, detailing how "down here, Du Fu found himself outside the civilization he knew. The indigenous people spoke a completely different dialect. It was a new world to him, physically and mentally." The ship continues its journey, with passengers in orange life vests visible on deck, some leaning on railings, others walking along the covered walkway. Michael Wood is later seen inside the ship’s cabin, observing the river through a window, reflecting on Du Fu’s experience, stating, "And this is where he stopped, the entrance to the Yangtze Gorges." The video concludes with an aerial view of a lush, forested island at the entrance of the Three Gorges, featuring traditional Chinese-style pavilions and temples, identified by subtitles as the place where Du Fu paused his journey, blending grand natural vistas with intimate human observation.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with sweeping aerial views of a vast, muddy river winding through lush, mountainous terrain, featuring a prominent green island. Michael Wood, a middle-aged man with graying hair, wearing a black jacket over a light blue shirt, stands on a traditional stone bridge with ornate railings. With a British accent, he gestures expressively as he describes the landscape, noting how it could hardly be more different from Chengdu, highlighting the 'giant gorges' and the 'great river as powerful as an ocean,' referencing Du Fu. He speaks about Du Fu’s life in exile—building a small farm, growing vegetables, keeping chickens, and writing 'hundreds of poems' prolifically. The scene transitions to a high-angle drone shot of the river curving around the island, with a bridge connecting to the shore, as Michael Wood's narration continues, reflecting that 'it was clear now' that Du Fu’s dream of becoming the emperor's minister and having his picture hung in the 'unicorn hall would never be fulfilled.' Chinese subtitles reference Du Fu’s poetic imagery, and lines from his 'Autumn Wastes' poem appear on screen in both Chinese and English. The camera then cuts to a close-up of Michael Wood from behind, gazing out at the misty mountains, before a wide aerial shot of the river valley, shrouded in mist. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Michael Wood, a middle-aged man with graying hair, seated at a table on a river cruise ship, gazing reflectively at a scenic mountainous landscape, his hands clasped. A white mug, papers, and a notebook rest on the table. Soft, traditional Chinese-style music with plucked strings and flutes begins, setting a contemplative mood. Michael Wood's voiceover starts, explaining that in spring 768, Du Fu gave his cottage to a neighbor and moved on, the reason unknown. Sweeping aerial shots of rugged, sunlit mountains are shown, overlaid with Chinese subtitles quoting Du Fu’s poem 'Traveling Night, Thoughts of Home': '官应老病休' (Official service should be retired due to old age and illness) and '飘飘何所似 天地一沙鸥' (What am I like, drifting? A lone seagull between heaven and earth), evoking a sense of exile and transience. Michael Wood, now speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, describes Du Fu as a wanderer navigating the Yangtze River with his family, a couple of servants, and a boatman, carrying all their worldly goods and precious poetry collections in a small boat. The music continues as an animated map, superimposed over a river view, traces Du Fu’s journey southward from Baidicheng to Changsha (Zhou) between 768–769 CE, marked by a red line along the Yangtze River and Dongting Lake. The map includes labeled cities like Xi’an, Chengdu, and Luzhou, with the text 'HEADING SOUTH 768-769 CE'. Michael Wood's voiceover explains this journey to Tanjou, today's Changsha. The scene then cuts to a modern river view with a cruise ship moving through calm waters, followed by a wide shot of a contemporary cityscape with tall buildings and a cargo barge. Michael Wood walks along the riverbank, turning to face the skyline. The lighting is bright and clear, suggesting daytime, blending historical reflection with modern context. He concludes, comparing Changsha to Casablanca after the fall of France, emphasizing its safety from northern conflicts, as the traditional music fades out.

Michael Wood reappears, now speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, describing Du Fu as a wanderer navigating the Yangtze River with his family and a boatman, carrying his precious poetry collections in a small boat. The narrative shifts to an animated map superimposed over a river view, tracing Du Fu’s journey southward from Baidicheng to Changsha (Zhou) between 768–769 CE, marked by a red line along the Yangtze River and Dongting Lake. The scene then cuts to a modern river view with a cruise ship moving through calm waters, followed by a wide shot of a contemporary cityscape with tall buildings and a cargo barge, as Michael Wood walks along the riverbank, turning to face the skyline. Subtitles compare Changsha to the wartime refuge of Cassis in France, emphasizing its safety from northern conflicts. The lighting is bright and clear, suggesting daytime, with a mix of natural and urban environments, and the atmosphere blends historical reflection with modern context.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of the traditional multi-tiered Du Fu Jiang Pavilion along a calm riverbank, with modern city skyscrapers in the hazy background. Michael Wood, dressed in a dark suit, stands by the river, speaking in a clear British accent about Du Fu’s historical connection to the site, explaining that the pavilion is built where the poet supposedly stayed. He recounts how Du Fu left his boat and rented a room, looking over the fisherman's market, and quotes Du Fu's reflections on his wandering life, stating, "For so long I've had no fixed abode... My children have grown up on the road. In every place I've left a house and a garden." Wood then notes that Changsha offered Du Fu a "fresh start." The scene transitions to aerial views of the lush park, followed by ground-level shots of local residents engaging in morning exercise, dancing rhythmically in colorful athletic wear to upbeat traditional Chinese music. Michael Wood's narration continues over the music, describing how Changsha locals exercise, sing, dance, and play music "first thing in the morning, and again after work." Close-ups show feet in sneakers stepping in sync, then pan to a couple performing under a pavilion: a woman sings a melodic Chinese song into a microphone, accompanied by a man playing a golden saxophone, their live music providing the vibrant soundtrack. Wood concludes that here, Du Fu's "mood lifted," and he found a "refugee" before the audio cuts. The video ends with a man riding a bicycle along a tree-lined path and a wide shot of the pavilion, emphasizing the blend of historical architecture and contemporary urban life under bright sunlight.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a serene performance by young artists in traditional Chinese attire, seated and standing along a grassy riverside under a bright blue sky. They play ancient Tang Dynasty instruments, including the pipa and flute, and sing in unison, their harmonious traditional Chinese music and poetry filling the air. As the camera captures close-ups of their focused expressions and intricate hand movements, Michael Wood's narration explains that this performance, based on Du Fu's 'Meeting Li Guinian South of the River,' reflects how the poet's work was set to music through collaboration. He highlights Prof. Yang Yu and her students' efforts to recreate the authentic sound of these Tang instruments. The traditional Chinese music and singing continue as Michael details the poem's story of Du Fu encountering an old musician, once a court star, now a refugee. The scene transitions to a wider shot, showing the performers on a stone path beside the calm river, with modern buildings in the background, beautifully blending historical art with contemporary urban life. Prof. Yang Yu then speaks in Mandarin, emphasizing the inseparable connection between Chinese poetry and music, noting that poetry often serves as lyrics and that the instruments being played were already highly popular during Du Fu's Tang Dynasty. The overall atmosphere is reflective, celebrating the revival of classical art through performance.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with sweeping aerial shots of a lush, green river valley, winding through dense forests under a hazy sky, accompanied by ethereal, melancholic background music. A male narrator, with a British accent, explains that "Heavy rains forced him to turn back," and that Du Fu, "Long a sufferer from asthma and diabetes, his health collapsed." Chinese subtitles simultaneously narrate his deteriorating health. The scene transitions to a close-up of sunlit water with submerged tree trunks, then to a golden-hued, misty horizon where the sun glows faintly, as the narrator continues, "His last poem is from the winter of 770. A thousand leagues from home, as he said." The narrative shifts to a man in a dark blue shirt, standing on a riverbank, energetically rowing a traditional wooden boat with a long oar, his face expressive and upward-looking. The boat, carrying two figures, glides calmly across the water, framed by golden-hued trees along the shore. The narrator adds, "Written on a sick bed in the boat, his final words are a valedictory unlike that of any other great artist." The camera pans to a sun-drenched cliffside, where light filters through the trees, creating a dreamlike haze, with subtle water sounds joining the music. The segment concludes with Prof. Stephen Owen, an older man with white hair, wearing a black shirt and teal scarf, speaking solemnly against a dark, textured blue background. In a distinct, solemn voice, he recites lines from Du Fu's poem: "On my journey, I am sick, and time closes in on me. This watery land engulfs the simple cottages in a mist. Maple shores, layer green summits. Excitement gone, now nothing troubles me. My ragged clothes have been patched every inch..." Chinese subtitles simultaneously quote these lines, emphasizing themes of sorrow, confinement, and longing, as the background music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Prof. Stephen Owen, an older man with white hair, wearing a black shirt and teal scarf, speaking solemnly against a dark, textured blue background. His reflective voice, with a distinct British accent, recites lines from Du Fu's poetry, stating, "I've got to know the customs of all the nine regions of China, and still the blood of battle flows, as it has for so long." Ethereal, melancholic background music plays as Chinese subtitles appear, referencing Du Fu. The scene transitions to a close-up of a wooden oar splashing vigorously in murky water, symbolizing movement and effort, while Prof. Owen's voice continues, "The sounds of armies stir to this day." This is followed by a serene shot of Michael Wood, in a gray sweater, seen from behind, gazing at a sunlit river with mountains in the distance, the golden light reflecting on the water’s surface, evoking contemplation and historical reflection. The camera then returns to Prof. Stephen Owen, who continues his reflective commentary, "I have achieved nothing, and my tears fall like rain." The scene shifts to a shimmering close-up of sunlight dancing on rippling water, then to a lush, sun-drenched canopy of green leaves, with the on-screen text 'ANDING - PINGJIANG' and '平江' identifying the location. The background music becomes more prominent and atmospheric. The final shots reveal a traditional Chinese courtyard with a skylight, where sunlight streams through, illuminating dust motes, and then a dark interior with wooden latticed doors and a hanging lantern. Over these historical settings, a male voice, identifiable as Michael Wood's, with a clear British accent, narrates, "Du Fu died in 770, in complete obscurity, not yet 60, but old before his time. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of a traditional Chinese building, its weathered brick walls and dark wooden door flanked by red lanterns, as Michael Wood, a middle-aged man with graying hair, walks towards and enters it. His clear, articulate voice with a British accent begins, explaining how "with the war they couldn't" and "they settled here to tend his grave." Inside, the dimly lit interior reveals a large circular stone carving on the wall, which Michael walks past. He continues, "But they saved the scrolls of his poems. And within a decade of his death, a selection was published to great acclaim." The camera follows him into a hall where a large, weathered stone statue of Du Fu, depicted with a long beard and traditional robes, holding a scroll, sits on a pedestal beneath a golden plaque inscribed '诗圣中' (Poet Sage of the Center). Michael Wood stands beside the statue, gesturing expressively as he speaks, quoting a critic, "Since the dawn of poetry, one critic wrote, there is no one like him." He elaborates on Du Fu's enduring legacy, stating that "over the next two centuries, he would be acknowledged by common consent as China's greatest poet." Chinese subtitles appear at the bottom, reinforcing the historical context of Du Fu’s posthumous recognition and his symbolic role in Chinese civilization, such as '在他死后不到十年 一部广受称赞的杜甫诗选出版了' and '他将会被大众公认为中国最伟大的诗人'. Michael Wood then emphasizes that "to call him that is to underplay his importance in Chinese culture because it limits his standing simply to that of a poet," drawing a comparison by noting, "There's no comparable figure in Western culture. Someone who, by chance as it turned out, came to embody not only the feelings, but the moral sensibility of a whole civilization." A subtle, reverent instrumental background music underscores the solemn and reflective atmosphere, highlighting the statue and the intricate wooden lattice doors and red drapery, underscoring the poet's enduring legacy.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a traditional Chinese building, its weathered brick walls and dark wooden door flanked by tall cypress trees under bright sunlight, with lens flares adding a dreamy quality. Traditional Chinese instrumental music, featuring plucked strings and flute-like sounds, begins to play. Children in yellow and black school uniforms with red scarves run up stone steps towards the entrance, transitioning into a courtyard where they gather in formation. A male narrator explains in English, "Here at his grave near Pinjiang in Hunan, they hold a ceremony for him on the traditional day when the Chinese commemorate the ancestors, tomb sweeping day." A young girl steps forward with a microphone and a paper, her clear, formal Chinese voice announcing, "学校扫杜甫墓词活动现在开始!" (School Du Fu Tomb Sweeping Ceremony now begins!) and then instructing, "全体肃立，向杜甫墓敬花篮！" (All stand solemnly, present flower baskets to Du Fu's tomb!). The children, some holding bouquets of white and green flowers, then move forward in an orderly line, placing their flowers at a stone monument or grave site and bowing respectfully. The English narrator continues, "Today, every child in China learns Du Fu's words in school," as muffled children's voices are heard chanting or reciting in Chinese. He lists themes from Du Fu's works, such as "The meeting with the old musician," "The spring scene in the ruined capital," "The dancer Lady Gongsun," and "His wife standing alone in the moonlit window." The scene shifts to an indoor setting labeled 'NEW TIEPING POETRY CLUB,' where adults sit around a long table in a rustic, open-air hall with brick walls and framed photographs, listening attentively to a speaker. The narrator concludes, "They're taught about his love for the common people, and also, of course, his loyalty to the state," as the traditional music continues and faint children's chanting is heard again, emphasizing the enduring legacy of Du Fu through education and communal remembrance, a sentiment also conveyed by the on-screen Chinese subtitles.
